decisions.context: `writs-hierarchy.test.js` asserts `.map(r => [r.writ.id, r.isChild])`. A deep hierarchy needs more than a boolean.

options.depth-number: Replace `isChild` with `depth: number` (0 for roots, 1+ for descendants).
options.tree-shape: Return the nested `WritTree` instead of a flat list; renderer walks it.

decisions.rationale: Minimal API change, generalizes cleanly, tests can assert `depth` directly, no boolean duplication. Renderer still iterates a flat list which pairs well with the existing table.

decisions.question: Does the detail view's children table also recurse to show descendants, or stay at direct-children?
decisions.context: The detail view has its own children table (line 443–471 of index.html). The brief says 'must drill down via details page' — implying the detail page is where drill-down happens.

options.detail-stays-direct: Detail view children table remains direct-children-only; the brief targets the main list.
options.detail-recurses-too: Extend the detail view's children table with the same deep-descendant UI.
options.detail-links-only: Keep detail children direct-only but add "see all" links for any child with its own subtree.

decisions.rationale: Brief is explicitly scoped to the writs table. Detail view already works as the drill-down target (D7). Extending it is scope creep — worth recording as an observation.

decisions.question: Does `LIMIT` become configurable (per-user preference or query param) or remain a code constant?
decisions.context: Today `LIMIT` is a bare `const` inside the IIFE. A user wanting 50 or 500 has no knob.

options.constant-100: Keep as a constant, just change the value.
options.url-query: Honor `?limit=N` from the URL (simple; no storage).
options.settings-ui: Add a page-level setting with persistence.

decisions.rationale: Brief asks only to bump the default; no configurability requested. URL param or settings is scope creep and generates new decisions (persistence, validation).

writs.title: Writs Generated from Observation Must depend on the originating writ

decisions.question: Which link kind should the observation-lift engine use for the dependency edge?
decisions.context: The Spider contributes a single precedence link kind (`spider.follows`) whose description says the source is a precedence-successor that cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state — this is exactly what the brief asks for. No other kind in the registry gates dispatch. The brief explicitly calls for the 'dependency semantic link type from the spider'.

options.spider-follows: Use the Spider-contributed `spider.follows` kind (only kind that gates trySpawn).
options.new-astrolabe-kind: Contribute a new astrolabe-scoped kind (e.g. `astrolabe.lifted-from`) alongside `spider.follows`.
options.no-kind: Create the link with a free-text label only, no registered kind.

decisions.rationale: The brief pre-empts this decision: it names the Spider's dependency link type as the required mechanism. `spider.follows` is the only kind that gates Spider dispatch; a label-only link would not hold the gate.

decisions.question: Which direction should the `spider.follows` edge run — new draft writ → brief, or brief → new draft writ?
decisions.context: Per the kind's registered description: 'source is a precedence-successor of the target; source cannot be dispatched until the target reaches a terminal state.' The brief wants the new (observation-lifted) writ to be held until the originating brief completes. Every in-tree test of the gate uses the dependent as the source.

options.lifted-to-brief: Source = newly-posted draft writ, Target = originating brief writ.
options.brief-to-lifted: Source = originating brief, Target = newly-posted draft writ.

decisions.rationale: Matches the kind's semantic direction and the universal test-file convention (`clerk.link(dependent, blocker, 'depends on', 'spider.follows')`). Reversing the direction would make the lifted writ not a precedence-successor and would not gate anything.

decisions.question: What free-text label should accompany the `spider.follows` kind on the new link?
decisions.context: Clerk's `link()` takes a free-text label that becomes part of the composite link id (after normalization). The label is load-bearing for human readability (rendered in writ-show) but not for gate evaluation — the gate keys on `kind`. Every existing in-tree site that pairs a label with `spider.follows` uses `'depends on'` (8 occurrences across `spider.test.ts` and `clockworks-retry.integration.test.ts`). No other pairing exists.

options.depends-on: Use `'depends on'` (existing codebase convention for this kind).
options.observation-of: Use `'observation of'` (describes the origin rather than the precedence).

decisions.rationale: Unanimous existing convention. 'Follows' would split vocabulary, and 'observation of' describes provenance (already captured by parentId) rather than the precedence relationship the link expresses.

decisions.question: How should the post + link pair be sequenced within the engine's loop?
decisions.context: The engine today iterates observations and calls `clerk.post` once per record. Adding a `clerk.link` call can be done per-record (post → link → post → link …) or in two passes (all posts, then all links). `clerk.link` requires both endpoints to exist, and both ordering strategies satisfy that since the brief parent already exists.

options.per-record: Interleave: after each successful `clerk.post`, immediately `clerk.link` the new writ to the brief.
options.two-pass: First pass posts all writs, second pass links them all.

decisions.rationale: Per-record keeps each observation's post/link atomic from the loop's perspective — a mid-loop failure leaves a clean tail (each earlier observation either fully posted+linked or fully untouched). Two-pass would invite a failure mode where some writs are posted but none are linked, which is strictly worse. The existing fail-fast contract generalises to per-record pairing without complication.

decisions.question: How should the engine behave when `clerk.link` throws (e.g. the `spider.follows` kind is not registered because Spider is not installed, or an internal Clerk error)?
decisions.context: Today the engine's fail-fast contract says: on the first `clerk.post` error, propagate; already-created drafts persist under the brief; the curator reconciles manually. Adding `clerk.link` introduces a second failure site per observation. Silent fallback (swallow the error and continue) would create unlinked drafts that are indistinguishable from legitimate child writs but without the promised gate — a silent degradation that hides misconfiguration (`spider.follows` missing = Spider not installed).

options.fail-fast: Propagate the error immediately, same as `clerk.post` failure. Earlier post+link pairs persist for a curator to reconcile.
options.swallow: Log the error and continue creating draft writs without links.
options.detect-skip: Probe `clerk.listKinds()` at engine start; skip the link step silently when `spider.follows` is absent.

decisions.rationale: Matches the Three Defaults' fail-loud preference and the engine's existing fail-fast posture. The absence of `spider.follows` is a misconfiguration (Spider not installed in a guild that uses astrolabe's plan-and-ship rig — which itself already requires Spider engines), not a legitimate state to degrade into.

decisions.question: Should astrolabe's plugin-level dependency on `spider` be promoted from `recommends` to `requires` as part of this change?
decisions.context: Astrolabe today declares `requires: ['stacks', 'clerk']` and `recommends: ['spider', …]` in `astrolabe.ts:270–271`, pinned by `supportkit.test.ts:40,44`. The architecture doc (`docs/architecture/apparatus/astrolabe.md:20`) contradicts the code and says `requires: [clerk, stacks, spider, loom, fabricator]`. The plan-and-ship rig template — astrolabe's sole production rig — already references Spider-owned engines (`draft`, `implement`, `review`, `revise`, `seal`) and the `patron-input` block type, so astrolabe cannot function at all without Spider. Reality already says Spider is required; the declaration is simply out of sync.

options.keep-recommends: Leave `requires: ['stacks', 'clerk']` unchanged; rely on fail-fast at link time.
options.promote-requires: Change `requires` to `['stacks', 'clerk', 'spider']` and remove `spider` from `recommends`; update `supportkit.test.ts` expectations.

decisions.rationale: Tightening the dependency list is an adjacent cleanup the brief did not ask for. The engine-level fail-loud behaviour (D5) is sufficient to surface misconfiguration at the moment it matters. Promoting the dependency is a sensible follow-up but would broaden the blast radius of this change (supportkit.test.ts expectations, startup behaviour in Spider-less guilds) without being load-bearing for the brief. Capturing the discrepancy as an observation (see obs-1) routes it through the normal follow-up pipeline.

decisions.question: Should the engine's `yields` shape change to expose the created link ids (or other link metadata) alongside `writIds`?
decisions.context: The engine currently yields `{ writIds: string[] }`. No engine downstream in the `plan-and-ship` template consumes this yield (verified by scanning `plan-and-ship.ts`). `clerk.link` returns a `WritLinkDoc` with a composite `id` field; accumulating those alongside `writIds` would mirror the post step's accounting.

options.add-linkIds: Add a parallel `linkIds: string[]` slot so observability symmetry with `writIds` is preserved.

decisions.rationale: Extending the yield shape is an API surface change with no consumer. Fail-loud (D5) gives sufficient visibility when something goes wrong, and the link ids are recoverable from the writ via `clerk.links(writId)`. Don't extend the surface without a caller.

decisions.question: Should the engine emit a link for each observation unconditionally, or only when the observation count exceeds some threshold / some future per-observation flag opts out?
decisions.context: Observations are the engine's sole input signal. There is no existing per-observation flag. The brief assumes the gate applies to every lifted writ ('the generated writs do not express this dependency'). Nothing in the existing `Observation` type carries a 'depends / doesn't depend' bit.

options.unconditional: Every lifted writ gets a `spider.follows` → brief link.
options.conditional-flag: Add an optional `dependsOnBrief?: boolean` field to `Observation` (default true) and honour it in the engine.

decisions.rationale: The brief frames this as a property of observation-lifted writs categorically, not a per-observation knob. Adding a field is a speculative surface expansion that nothing asks for. If a future concern surfaces a 'this observation doesn't depend on the brief' need, it can be added then.

decisions.question: How much of the engine/apparatus documentation should move alongside this change?
decisions.context: Three places carry load-bearing prose about the engine: the engine's leading JSDoc (`observation-lift.ts:1–33`), the astrolabe README engines table row (`README.md:147`), and the architecture doc (`docs/architecture/apparatus/astrolabe.md:100–107, 132, 156`). All three say the parent-child relationship is the sole audit trail — that sentence is no longer accurate once the engine also emits an outbound `spider.follows` link.

options.engine-only: Update just the engine docstring; leave README and architecture doc to a follow-up.
options.all-three: Update the engine docstring, the README row, and the architecture doc together so the three stay synchronised.
options.full-audit: Update the three plus any other prose that mentions observation-lift (e.g. `plan-and-ship.ts` header comment, any in-progress docs).

decisions.rationale: Keeps the engine's contract, the user-facing API doc, and the architecture doc in lockstep. `engine-only` guarantees the known discrepancy grows; `full-audit` includes incidental surfaces (the `plan-and-ship.ts` header describes placement rather than the engine's audit-trail claim) that don't carry the same load-bearing assertion. Updating these three together mirrors how prior astrolabe changes have moved.

decisions.question: Do the Reckoner and the Lattice ship as two packages or one combined package?
decisions.context: The brief introduces two named apparatus with distinct purposes: the Lattice is the general-purpose messaging substrate open to multiple future emitters (anima completion reports, coinmaster, vision-keeper, etc.), and the Reckoner is one narrowly-scoped emitter for MVP. `clockworks-retry-apparatus` is precedent for a standalone observer package layered on Spider+Clerk.

options.two-packages: Ship `@shardworks/lattice-apparatus` and `@shardworks/reckoner-apparatus` as separate npm packages.
options.one-package: Ship a single `@shardworks/lattice-apparatus` package that includes both the substrate and the Reckoner observer.

decisions.rationale: Lattice is designed to outlive the MVP Reckoner (multiple future emitters planned); keeping them separate mirrors the clockworks-retry precedent, makes the 'Lattice is substrate, not policy' line explicit, and lets a guild install one without the other.

decisions.question: When the Reckoner observes a writ entering `stuck`, should it emit on every stuck transition or only when the stuck is terminal-for-the-patron (retryable=false OR retry-cap-exhausted)?
decisions.context: `SpiderWritStatus.retryable` classifies engine-failure stucks as transient (clockworks-retry will requeue) vs definitional. `MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS = 2` caps retries. The concluded triggers click (c-mob1vyb2) describes the MVP triggers as `commission reaches terminal non-success: stuck / failed / retry-cap-exhausted` — explicitly contrasting 'terminal non-success' with retryable transients.

options.only-terminal-non-success: Emit only when the writ entering `stuck` has `status.spider.retryable !== true` OR rigs-for-writ count >= MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS. Also emit on entry to `failed`. Match exactly the complement of clockworks-retry's requeue condition.
options.fire-on-every-stuck-and-fail: Emit on every entry into stuck or failed; let the channel (or the patron) filter out transients.
options.fire-on-every-with-metadata: Emit on every stuck/failed, include retryable metadata in pulse.context so downstream can suppress.

decisions.rationale: Brief prescribes the 'terminal non-success' framing. Firing on every transient stuck would produce 2+ pings per self-healing failure — exactly the noise the retry clockwork was built to silence.

decisions.question: How should `pulse.triggerType` values be namespaced?
decisions.context: The pulse-shape click (c-mobzl35x) says trigger_type is a namespaced string extensible by future producers. Clerk's link-kind registry uses `{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}` with hard validation at kit registration.

options.namespace-by-emitter: `reckoner.writ-stuck`, `reckoner.writ-failed`, `reckoner.queue-drained` — `{pluginId}.{kebab-suffix}`, same grammar Clerk uses for link kinds.
options.event-centric-no-prefix: `writ.stuck`, `writ.failed`, `queue.drained` — semantic names, collisions possible between emitters.
options.qualified-by-writ-type: `mandate.stuck`, `mandate.failed` — ties pulse type to writ type; rigid.

decisions.rationale: Aligns with the existing link-kind namespacing convention; avoids collisions when future emitters (coinmaster, anima, vision-keeper) add trigger types; keeps `pulse.source` and `pulse.triggerType` distinct axes (who vs what).

decisions.question: What should `pulse.linkUrl` contain for writ-scoped pulses in MVP?
decisions.context: Pulses carry an optional `link_url` so push channels can deep-link into guild state. The Oculus serves writ pages at `/pages/writs/` but the public base URL is not known to emitters — the Oculus reads `port` from its own config, and guilds run on operator machines (no canonical public URL). The pulse-shape click leaves `link_url` as nullable.

options.leave-nullable: Emit pulses with `linkUrl: null` in MVP. Channels render without a link.
options.require-web-base-url-config: Add `lattice.webBaseUrl` config; concat; fail loud if unset and a writ-scoped pulse would need a link.

decisions.rationale: A local URL is broken for remote patrons; a required config is scope creep for MVP; nullable + channel-aware rendering ships the commission without a half-working link mechanism. Future decision once 'guild public web base URL' is an actual concept.

decisions.question: What is the shape of the kit contribution type that channels register through?
decisions.context: The Lattice needs to accept channel implementations from external kits (Discord today, ntfy/Pushover/etc. later). A channel has (a) a type name, (b) per-guild instance configuration (webhook URL env-var, headers), (c) a send(pulse) method.

options.factory-kit: Kits contribute `latticeChannels: [{ type: string, create(instanceConfig): LatticeChannel }]`. Lattice reads guild config for the enabled types/configs and calls factories to materialize instances. Channel implements `{ send(pulse): Promise<DeliveryOutcome> }`.
options.direct-channel-instances: Kits contribute fully-instantiated channel objects; Lattice uses them as-is. No per-guild instance config.
options.apparatus-per-channel: Each channel is its own apparatus with its own lifecycle; Lattice discovers them via `guild().apparatus()`.

decisions.rationale: Instance configuration is per-guild (one Discord webhook URL, maybe one ntfy topic), not per-kit; factories pass config at creation time. Kit-based registration (vs full apparatus) keeps simple channels simple — only the producer (Lattice) has lifecycle.

decisions.question: Where does the Discord webhook channel live — bundled in the Lattice package or as a standalone channel kit?
decisions.context: The Lattice is intended to be substrate; its channels are open-ended. Bundling proves the kit-contribution path works but couples the substrate to one push surface's dependencies.

options.bundled-in-lattice-supportkit: Discord channel is contributed via `lattice-apparatus`'s own `supportKit.latticeChannels`. One npm install gets you Lattice + Discord.
options.separate-kit-package: Ship `@shardworks/lattice-discord-kit` as a standalone kit package. Operators install it alongside the Lattice to get push.

decisions.rationale: Proves the channel abstraction works end-to-end with the first-party channel as an external contributor — exactly the test the concluded channel click wanted. Keeps the Lattice core free of channel-specific deps. Operators who only want CLI inbox (no Discord) don't install lattice-discord-kit.

decisions.question: How should the Reckoner determine 'queue drained'?
decisions.context: The click specifies 'check remaining runnable work, emit drain pulse if empty'. Spider's `trySpawn` looks for writs in `open` of dispatchable types and holds off if `running`/`blocked` rigs fill the concurrency budget. Stuck writs may be retryable (will flip back to open) or terminal-for-now.

options.simple-count: Drain = `0 open writs` AND `0 rigs in (running | blocked)`. Ignore stuck — stuck writs either flip back to open (retry) or stay stuck (human attention needed, queue is effectively drained from the auto-dispatcher's view).
options.include-retryable-stuck: Also count stuck-with-retryable-true-under-cap as 'pending runnable work'; only drained if those are also zero.
options.mirror-spider-exactly: Duplicate Spider's `trySpawn` filter logic (dispatchable types + concurrency budget) inside Reckoner.

decisions.rationale: Retryable-stuck will transition back to open within one retry-clockwork tick; false-positive drain pulses in that millisecond window are self-correcting (the re-open is another terminal-transition signal). Mirroring Spider's filter binds Reckoner to Spider internals unnecessarily. Simple count is correct for the patron-facing 'nothing left to work on' intent.

decisions.question: What does the Lattice do when a channel's `send()` fails (network error, webhook 5xx, non-2xx)?
decisions.context: The rate-limit click (c-mob1w0ql) says 'nothing for MVP' and defers retry/digest logic. Pulse.deliveryState has `failed` as a value. Discord webhook has roughly 30 req/min quota but MVP volume should be under that.

options.best-effort-one-shot: Try once per channel. On failure, mark `deliveryState: 'failed'` (or keep pending for a single retry on boot). No retry loop.
options.per-channel-retry-with-backoff: Retry 2-3 times with exponential backoff before giving up.
options.persistent-retry-queue: Background worker scans `failed` pulses and re-attempts periodically.

decisions.rationale: Matches the rate-limit click's 'nothing for MVP' stance and aligns with the Three Defaults' fail-loud preference. A failed webhook leaves a `failed` row visible in the CLI inbox — operator sees it and investigates. Retry policy is exactly the kind of Lattice-side feature the click defers.

decisions.question: Which CDC phase should the Reckoner's writ observer run in?
decisions.context: Stacks offers Phase 1 (cascade, failOnError:true, inside the triggering transaction) and Phase 2 (notification, failOnError:false, post-commit). The Reckoner writes into a different book (`lattice/pulses`) and calls a different apparatus's API, so there's no re-entry on the watched book; but pulse emission is a layered policy observation, not a data-integrity cascade.

options.phase-2-notification: Register with `failOnError: false`. Phase 2 runs after commit; failures are logged but don't roll back the writ's stuck/failed write.
options.phase-1-cascade: Register with `failOnError: true`. Any pulse emission failure would rollback the writ's phase transition.

decisions.rationale: Matches clockworks-retry's reasoning exactly: a pulse is a layered notification; failure should never void the underlying writ state change. Phase 1 would tie the writ's liveness to the Lattice being healthy, which inverts the substrate relationship.

decisions.question: How does the Lattice dispatch a pulse to channels — synchronously inside `emit()` or asynchronously via a CDC watcher on the pulses book?
decisions.context: The Reckoner is itself inside a Phase 2 observer when it calls `lattice.emit()`. Channel sends hit the network (Discord webhook POST). A sync path blocks the observer for hundreds of ms per pulse; an async path via CDC makes pulse emission fast and dispatch naturally restart-safe.

options.async-via-cdc-plus-startup-scan: `emit()` writes the pulse with `deliveryState: 'pending'`. A Phase 2 watcher on `lattice/pulses` picks up creates and dispatches to every channel, then patches delivery state. On startup, the Lattice scans for `deliveryState === 'pending'` rows and dispatches them.
options.sync-in-emit: `emit()` writes the pulse and then immediately awaits every channel send before returning, updating delivery state inline.
options.async-worker-poll: `emit()` writes the pulse; a background interval poller picks up pending rows.

decisions.rationale: Keeps emission fast (no network in the writ CDC observer path); makes failures survive process crashes (pending pulses get re-dispatched on restart); reuses the CDC machinery already proven by clockworks-retry. Polling is the same pattern with weaker real-time properties.

decisions.question: How should the Discord channel render a pulse in the webhook payload?
decisions.context: Discord webhooks accept either a plain `{ content: string }` or a rich `{ embeds: [...] }` with title, description, color, fields, and a URL. Pulse carries title + summary + optional linkUrl + context JSON.

options.rich-embed: Post `{ embeds: [{ title, description, url?, color, fields }] }`. Color-code by triggerType (stuck/failed/drained); fields expose writ short-id and trigger type.

decisions.rationale: Embeds render much better on mobile, support color-coding, and let us include structured context without sacrificing the one-line headline. The content-size savings are negligible.

decisions.question: Does the Reckoner need guild configuration?
decisions.context: Clockworks-retry ships with no user-facing config — cap is a compile-time constant; observation is autowired. Reckoner's trigger set is the prescribed MVP set with no documented knobs.

options.no-config: Reckoner reads no config; installing the plugin turns it on; removing it turns it off. Matches clockworks-retry.
options.trigger-type-allowlist: Let operators pass an allowlist of enabled trigger types.

decisions.rationale: Single source of truth is the install; no hidden flags; aligned with clockworks-retry's precedent. Future trigger-gating (quiet hours, per-source suppression) belongs on the Lattice's delivery side per the rate-limit click's principle.

decisions.question: What is the shape of the Lattice's guild configuration and how are channel secrets referenced?
decisions.context: The channel click (c-mob1vyyi) explicitly defers 'webhook URLs, secrets, config schema' to the planning rig. copilot/src/index.ts uses `tokenEnvVar: string` to name the env var holding the secret. Lattice needs to know which channel factories to instantiate for this guild and pass each its instance config.

options.channels-array-with-env-var-refs: `lattice.channels: Array<{ type: string, ...instanceConfig }>` where per-channel shape is defined by the channel kit. Discord entry: `{ type: 'discord-webhook', webhookUrlEnvVar: 'DISCORD_WEBHOOK_URL' }`. Secrets come from the named env var at send time.
options.keyed-by-type: `lattice.channels: Record<type, instanceConfig>` — keyed map. Limits you to one instance per type.
options.direct-secret-in-config: Store webhook URLs inline in guild.json. Rejected on security grounds.

decisions.rationale: Matches copilot's existing env-var-name pattern; an array (not a map) supports multiple instances of the same type (e.g. two Discord channels for two rooms); never writes secrets to guild.json. Per-channel instanceConfig schema is defined by the channel kit (Lattice just hands it through).

decisions.question: What prefix do pulse IDs use?
decisions.context: The framework convention is `{letter}-{base36ts}-{hex_random}` (writs `w-*`, clicks `c-*`, rigs `rig-*`). `generateId(prefix, sections)` from nexus-core produces them.
decisions.rationale: Matches the framework's one-letter prefix convention; keeps IDs short in CLI output and Discord embeds; `p-` is unambiguous among `w-`, `c-`, `rig-`.

decisions.question: Does the Lattice ship an Oculus page for pulses in MVP?
decisions.context: The response-surface click (c-mob1w04q) prescribes Discord + CLI read-only. No Oculus page is mentioned. Clerk and Ratchet both contribute Oculus pages via `supportKit.pages`.

options.defer-page: Ship MVP without an Oculus page; Lattice just declares `recommends: ['oculus']` for future.
options.minimal-hook-routes-only: Ship API routes but no static pages.

decisions.rationale: Brief concluded the response surface as Discord + CLI only. An Oculus page is out of scope, not a tiny add. Ship narrow.

decisions.question: Does the Reckoner declare `clockworks-retry` as a hard `requires` or a soft `recommends`?
decisions.context: Reckoner needs `MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS` + rigs-count to decide whether a stuck is terminal-for-the-patron. Without clockworks-retry installed, every stuck is terminal (nothing will auto-resume it) so Reckoner still works, just with a different decision tree.

options.recommends: Declare `recommends: ['clockworks-retry']`. At emit time, resolve the cap via `guild().apparatus<ClockworksRetryApi>('clockworks-retry')?.maxAttempts`. If the apparatus is absent, treat every stuck as terminal (no retry mechanism exists, so every stuck is patron-facing).
options.requires: Declare `requires: ['clockworks-retry']`. Reckoner won't start without it.
options.hardcode-the-cap: Duplicate `MAX_RETRY_ATTEMPTS = 2` as a local constant; don't depend on the other apparatus at all.

decisions.rationale: A guild with no retry clockwork still benefits from Reckoner — it should just fire on every stuck. `requires` would force retry policy to always ship alongside notifications; `hardcode` duplicates a truth that belongs in one place. Same pattern the clockworks-retry apparatus uses toward Spider.

decisions.question: What tool names expose the CLI inbox, and what flags does `pulse-list` take?
decisions.context: The response-surface click prescribes `nsg pulse list`, `nsg pulse list --live`, `nsg pulse show <id>`. CLI tools are auto-grouped by hyphen prefix — `pulse-list` → `nsg pulse list`, `pulse-show` → `nsg pulse show`.

options.two-tools-plus-flag: Two tools: `pulse-list` (with `--live` boolean flag, `--since` date filter, `--limit` + `--offset`) and `pulse-show` (id arg).
options.three-tools: Three tools: `pulse-list`, `pulse-list-live`, `pulse-show`. Would auto-group to `nsg pulse list-live`, breaking the click's prescribed `--live` flag syntax.

decisions.rationale: Brief prescribes this exact shape. The CLI's auto-grouping mechanics make the two-tool-plus-flag layout produce the prescribed command forms.

decisions.question: What is the default time window for `pulse list`?
decisions.context: Concluded click c-mob1w04q: `nsg pulse list` shows 'recent pulses, default last 24h'.

options.24-hour-default-with-since-override: Default filter `createdAt >= now - 24h`. Override with `--since <iso>`, `--all` to disable.
options.no-default-window: Return most-recent N (paginated) with no time filter.
options.7-day-default: Wider default for less active guilds.

decisions.rationale: Brief prescribes 24h default. `--since` and `--all` are standard escape hatches.

decisions.question: What is the default sort order for `pulse list`?
decisions.context: Existing `writ-list` and `click-list` sort by `createdAt desc` (newest first) with a default limit of 20.

options.newest-first: `createdAt desc`, limit 20 by default. Matches peer list tools.

decisions.rationale: Matches peer list tools; patron wants the most recent alerts at the top.

decisions.question: How does `--live` determine whether a pulse's referent condition still holds?
decisions.context: The concluded click says 'pulses whose referent condition still holds — derived by joining to writ state'. Writ-scoped pulses (writ-stuck, writ-failed) have `writId`; drain pulses do not.

options.join-to-writ-phase: For pulses with writId, resolve the writ and include only if its current phase ∈ {stuck, failed}. Drain pulses are excluded from `--live` (no writ to join).
options.evaluator-per-trigger-type: Register a live-check per trigger type; `reckoner.queue-drained` → re-check current drain state at query time.
options.include-all-without-writid: Writ pulses joined as above; non-writ pulses always included.

decisions.rationale: Simplest correct reading of 'referent condition still holds' for the MVP trigger set. Drain pulses, by their nature, represent a transient-past event (the moment the queue went dry); 'is the queue still dry' is re-checked by the next terminal transition, not by the CLI. Exclusion is cleaner than a second evaluator path.

decisions.question: What value does `pulse.source` carry and who sets it?
decisions.context: The pulse-shape click calls `source` an emitter identifier ('reckoner, coinmaster, anima, ...'). Spider/Clerk convention keys plugin-owned slots by plugin id.

options.emitter-plugin-id: `source = pluginId` of the emitter; set by the emitter when calling `lattice.emit()`. Lattice does not forge or validate it.
options.lattice-derived-role: Lattice computes source from some runtime role descriptor.

decisions.rationale: Plugin-id-keyed ownership is the established pattern for status sub-slots. Emitters are the authority on their identity. Lattice trusts the emitter the way Stacks trusts book owners.

decisions.question: What format is `pulse.summary` — plain text, markdown, or structured?
decisions.context: The pulse-shape click: 'summary (few lines of human context)'. Separate `context` field holds structured JSON. Channels render to Discord (supports markdown), CLI (plain), future ntfy/email.

options.plain-text: Summary is plain text. Channels can do channel-specific rendering on top; no one has to parse markdown.
options.markdown: Summary is markdown. Channels render accordingly; CLI renders raw.
options.structured-fields: Break summary into typed sub-fields (cause, resolution, writ-title).

decisions.rationale: Plain degrades gracefully across channel backends. Structured data already lives in `context`; duplicating it as typed summary fields blurs the split. Channels that want richer rendering key off `context` plus `triggerType`.

decisions.question: Should the Reckoner emit for every writ that enters stuck/failed, or only for commission-root writs (parentId absent)?
decisions.context: Writs form trees; child failure cascades to parent via Clerk's Phase 1 downward cascade. If Reckoner fires on every writ in the tree, one commission failure produces N pulses. The concluded triggers click says 'commission reaches terminal non-success' — 'commission' in the guild metaphor is the patron-facing mandate, typically the root writ.

options.roots-only: Emit only when the transitioning writ has no parentId. Cascaded root failures produce exactly one pulse per commission.
options.emit-for-all: Emit on every stuck/failed transition regardless of tree depth. Tree-deep failures produce multiple pulses.
options.leaf-only-first-mover: Emit on the leaf-most failing writ in a cascade; skip propagated parent transitions.

decisions.rationale: Matches 'commission' framing in the brief; avoids N-pulse noise per cascading failure; simplest rule with a clean predicate (`!writ.parentId`). Child-writ detail is recoverable via `context` at emit time (name the leaf cause in the summary).

decisions.question: What is the exhaustive set of MVP trigger types the Reckoner emits?
decisions.context: Concluded triggers click enumerates the MVP set as blocked + drain; no delivered-side (B) triggers; no cancellation signals.

options.collapsed-single-type: One type `reckoner.writ-terminal` with the phase in context.

decisions.rationale: Brief prescribes blocked + drain only. Cancellation is patron-initiated (no surprise signal). Distinct types let channels color-code or suppress per-type.

decisions.question: Should the Lattice and Reckoner be added to the default guild plugin set in `nsg init`?
decisions.context: The 'Standard Guild' docs table notes itself provisional. Clockworks-retry is not yet in the default set; ratchet is, oculus is. Adding Lattice+Reckoner to the default requires a change to the init path.

options.opt-in-only: Keep MVP opt-in; operators install explicitly. Matches clockworks-retry precedent.
options.default-both: Add both to `nsg init`'s default plugin list so every new guild gets notifications.
options.default-lattice-only: Add Lattice to default (substrate is harmless without channels configured); leave Reckoner opt-in.

decisions.rationale: MVP is a new capability; exercising it in real guilds before defaulting it avoids premature lock-in. The brief does not prescribe default-set inclusion. This can be revisited in a later commission once the apparatus prove out.

decisions.question: What permission level do `pulse-list` and `pulse-show` declare?
decisions.context: Peer read tools: `writ-list` uses `permission: 'clerk:read'`, `writ-show` same. The permission string is `{pluginId}:{level}`; the Loom resolves role grants.
decisions.rationale: Matches the clerk/ratchet convention for list/show tools. Guild operator grants `lattice:read` to any role that should see pulses (patron, steward, reviewer).

decisions.question: On Reckoner startup, should it scan for pre-existing stuck/failed writs and emit pulses for them?
decisions.context: If the Reckoner is newly installed, the writs book may already contain stuck/failed writs that never triggered pulses. On restart after a crash, transient state between observation and pulse write may leave gaps.

options.no-backfill: Only react to CDC events from now forward. No startup scan. Keeps pulses aligned with their triggering transition; no phantom pulses for writs that stuck hours ago.
options.backfill-recent: Scan stuck/failed writs with `resolvedAt > now - 24h` on startup; emit missing pulses.
options.mark-on-writ-then-skip: Write a 'pulse emitted' marker to `writ.status.reckoner` so re-emission can be suppressed.

decisions.rationale: Pulses are immutable event records that fire on transitions (per concluded shape click). A stuck writ that already exists is a state, not a transition. Restart doesn't double-ping. The 'mark-on-writ' path reintroduces shadow state that c-mobzl35x explicitly rejected.

decisions.question: What does the tool handler for `pulse-list` / `pulse-show` return — raw structured data or rendered text?
decisions.context: `writ-list`, `click-list`, `writ-show` return raw JSON-serializable objects. `click-tree` returns rendered ASCII text (or JSON with `format: 'json'`). Pulses are flat records (not a tree).

options.raw-arrays-like-writ-list: Return `PulseDoc[]` / `PulseDoc`. CLI stringifies; MCP returns typed payload; human reads JSON.
options.rendered-text-default-json-flag: Render to ASCII table by default; `--format json` for machine consumption.
options.both-via-format-param: Add a `format: 'table' | 'json'` param with table default.

decisions.rationale: Consistency with peer list tools. A rendered view is a separate concern that can live in a CLI subcommand or in Oculus later. Rendering logic in a tool handler leaks presentation into the API surface.

decisions.question: What methods does the `LatticeApi` (provides) expose?
decisions.context: Other apparatus expose read, query, and mutation methods through their `provides` interface. Reckoner must be able to emit; the CLI tools need list/show; future emitters need emit.

options.emit-only-cli-reads-from-stacks: `emit()` only on the API. CLI tools read directly via `stacks.readBook('lattice', 'pulses')`.
options.full-crud-including-update: Add delete/update — but pulses are immutable per c-mobzl35x.

decisions.rationale: Mirrors Clerk/Ratchet style for a clean typed API surface; lets CLI tools depend on the Lattice API (stable) rather than on the Stacks book ownership convention (implementation detail). Update/delete deliberately absent because pulses are immutable.

decisions.question: What goes into `pulse.context` per trigger type?
decisions.context: Pulse shape (c-mobzl35x) leaves `context` as 'nullable JSON blob'. Channels and downstream consumers use it for trigger-specific structured data beyond the human-readable summary.

options.trigger-typed-payloads: `reckoner.writ-stuck`: { writShortId, writPhase, writTitle, writType, stuckCause, retryable, detail }. `reckoner.writ-failed`: { writShortId, writTitle, writType, resolution, childFailures? }. `reckoner.queue-drained`: { drainedAt, lastTerminalWritId }.
options.freeform-emitter-choice: Each emitter puts whatever it likes; no enforced shape.

decisions.rationale: Small structured payloads keyed to the triggerType give channels material to render (color, fields, link resolution) without re-parsing the summary. Documented per-type shape is cheap now and unlocks richer Discord embeds and future consumers. `null-for-mvp` forecloses Discord embed fields.

decisions.question: What pagination defaults does `pulse-list` use?
decisions.context: `writ-list` defaults to `limit: 20` with optional offset. Click-list same.

options.limit-20-offset-0: Match peer list tools — `limit: 20, offset: 0` defaults.
options.larger-default: Default to 50 or 100 (more pulses per page).
options.no-pagination-return-all-24h: Just return everything in the 24h window.

decisions.rationale: Consistency with peer tools; prevents runaway responses when the guild is noisy; `--limit`/`--offset` overrides available.

writs.title: `PlanDoc.generatedWritId` is documented as legacy but still on the type

decisions.question: Should `generatedWritId` (both the typed field and the plan-detail renderer) be removed entirely, or kept to preserve display of historic plandocs that may still carry the field?
decisions.context: The field is never written by current code (the only writer, `spec-publish`, was deleted in `w-mobvh1t8-2f1573ccbe43`). Its stated purpose is solely to let historic plandocs deserialize and render a 'Mandate Writ' link on the plan-detail page. The brief frames the follow-up as 'contingent on whether historic plandocs are still being read in production'. Codified policy `c-mo1yb8nf` prefers removal to deprecation; the PlanDoc `[key: string]: unknown` index signature ensures historic documents continue to deserialize without error even after the named field is removed.

options.full-removal: Delete the TS field, the renderer, and all documentation references. Historic plandocs still deserialize (via the index signature) but the Mandate Writ anchor no longer renders.
options.status-quo: Keep both the typed field and the renderer as-is, preserving display of the Mandate Writ link on any historic plandoc that carries the field.
options.partial-renderer-only: Remove the typed field but keep the renderer (the renderer's runtime access tolerates the missing type). The field would become an untyped leftover accessed only via the index signature — same net UI behavior as status-quo but with a type-level cleanup.
options.partial-field-only: Remove the renderer but keep the typed field. The stored data would remain accessible via the API but no UI would display it.

decisions.rationale: Three Defaults (prefer removal to deprecation, per `c-mo1yb8nf`); the field has no current writer, and the `[key: string]: unknown` index signature prevents deserialization regressions for any historic plandoc still in storage.

decisions.question: After the 'Mandate Writ' renderer is removed, how should the coupled regression-guard test assertion (currently requiring `>= 2` canonical `/pages/writs/?writ=` anchors) be updated?
decisions.context: The test at `astrolabe.test.js:574–582` pins the count of canonical anchors at `>= 2` with an explanatory comment naming both the brief-writ and mandate-writ anchors. Removing the renderer drops the count to 1 and fails the assertion. The preceding test (`'writ deep-links target the canonical Clerk writs page path'`, lines 561–572) already guards the URL-shape regression independently.

options.tighten-to-exact-one: Change the assertion to `matches.length === 1` (or `>= 1`) and rewrite the comment to describe only the single brief-writ anchor. Pins the new invariant explicitly.
options.weaken-to-at-least-one: Change the assertion to `>= 1`. Preserves flexibility in case the plan-detail card gains other canonical writ anchors later.
options.delete-second-test: Delete the second `it(...)` block entirely. The first test (`writ deep-links target the canonical …`) already guards the URL-shape regression this block was layered on top of; the explicit two-anchor count is now vestigial.

decisions.rationale: The codebase convention for regression-guard tests is to pin invariants explicitly (see the same file's `toFixed(4)` and bare-locale assertions for precedent). A `=== 1` form encodes the post-removal state and catches accidental re-introduction of a 'Mandate Writ' renderer.

decisions.question: Should the `[key: string]: unknown` index signature on `PlanDoc` (types.ts line 15) be kept, given that one of its load-bearing functions has been to tolerate legacy fields like `generatedWritId`?
decisions.context: The index signature currently lets historic plandocs deserialize even if they carry field names the named interface does not declare. Removing `generatedWritId` without the index signature would mean historic documents with that key no longer satisfy the declared shape. The signature is also a general-purpose extension point across `PlanDoc` — not solely for `generatedWritId`.

options.keep: Leave the index signature unchanged. Historic plandocs continue to deserialize cleanly; any future legacy-field drift is absorbed silently.
options.remove: Drop the index signature together with `generatedWritId`. Tightens the type but forces every reader to refactor if they inadvertently touched unknown fields, and removes the safety net for other historic fields.

decisions.rationale: Removing the signature is a larger, separate cleanup that would affect readers beyond the `generatedWritId` path; keeping it preserves historic-plandoc deserialization without blocking this commission. Three Defaults removal applies to unused code, not to load-bearing structural escape hatches.

decisions.question: Should the `spec-publish`-era legacy doc comment (currently explaining why the field exists) be deleted along with the field, or rewritten into a historical note somewhere?
decisions.context: The comment block at `types.ts:45–50`, the README line 67 inline comment, and the architecture-doc lines 199–203 explain that the field is a residue of the retired `spec-publish` engine and survives only to render historic plandocs. Once the field is removed, the comment has no subject; the only question is whether a successor comment (e.g., a `HISTORY` note explaining that plandocs from before date X may carry a `generatedWritId` key) should be preserved.

options.delete-all: Remove the field and its comments in both source (`types.ts`) and both docs (`README.md`, `architecture/astrolabe.md`). No historical note is added; the predecessor commission's changelog / writ history is the sole record.
options.delete-field-keep-history-note: Remove the field but add a short `HISTORY` or `Legacy keys` note near the PlanDoc interface (or at the bottom of the README) documenting that pre-X plandocs may carry a `generatedWritId` key that readers can access via the index signature.
options.rewrite-comment: Keep some form of comment near where the field used to live, explaining to future readers what the deleted field was and when it was removed.

decisions.rationale: The commit history + the predecessor commission writ `w-mobvh1t8-2f1573ccbe43` + this commission's own writ are sufficient historical record. A free-floating `HISTORY` comment inside the type declaration would itself become stale and would accumulate over time with no mechanism for retirement. Three Defaults: prefer removal to deprecation applies to the comment too.

decisions.question: What name should the extracted constant take?
decisions.context: The file already has one exported single-source-of-truth constant: `CASCADE_PARENT_TERMINATION_RESOLUTION` (`clerk.ts:85`). The new constant plays two overlapping roles: it is the one member of `BUILTIN_TYPES` and the fallback value of `resolveDefaultType()`. The name should read sensibly in both call sites.

options.BUILTIN_WRIT_TYPE: `const BUILTIN_WRIT_TYPE = 'mandate'` — emphasizes the builtin role; the default-fallback role is implicit.
options.MANDATE_TYPE: `const MANDATE_TYPE = 'mandate'` — names the value directly; does not capture intent.

decisions.rationale: `BUILTIN_TYPES` is the anchor site — the constant is declared as a member of that Set — so the shared `BUILTIN_` prefix keeps the naming coherent at the declaration. Reads naturally at both call sites (`new Set([BUILTIN_WRIT_TYPE])` and `config.defaultType ?? BUILTIN_WRIT_TYPE`). Three Defaults default: closest fit to existing convention.

decisions.question: Should the constant be exported from the package (re-exported via `index.ts`)?
decisions.context: The brief explicitly says "a single exported constant." The existing precedent `CASCADE_PARENT_TERMINATION_RESOLUTION` is declared `export const` in `clerk.ts` and re-exported from `index.ts`, making it available to tests and downstream consumers. A module-private `const` would satisfy the two internal call sites but not the brief's wording.

options.export-from-index: Declare `export const BUILTIN_WRIT_TYPE = 'mandate'` in `clerk.ts` and add it to the re-export block in `index.ts`, matching the `CASCADE_PARENT_TERMINATION_RESOLUTION` pattern exactly.
options.module-private: Declare `const BUILTIN_WRIT_TYPE = 'mandate'` without export; both call sites are local to `clerk.ts` so nothing external needs it.

decisions.rationale: Brief-pre-emption: the brief explicitly says "single exported constant." Also matches the sibling precedent — the builtin-type name is exactly the kind of value downstream tests or external tools might want to import rather than hardcode.

decisions.question: Should `BUILTIN_TYPES` remain a `Set` built from the constant, or be replaced with the constant directly?
decisions.context: `BUILTIN_TYPES` is used in two places — line 119 and line 738 — both iterated via `[...BUILTIN_TYPES].map(...)` to seed the merged map. A `Set` is the only reason the builtin-iteration code reads the way it does; if the set is always of cardinality one, callers could use the constant directly.

options.keep-set: Keep `const BUILTIN_TYPES = new Set([BUILTIN_WRIT_TYPE])` — minimum change, iteration code unchanged, and the structure stays ready to hold more builtins if one is ever added.
options.inline-constant: Drop `BUILTIN_TYPES` entirely and inline the constant in the two iteration sites (e.g., `mergedWritTypes.set(BUILTIN_WRIT_TYPE, { source: 'builtin' })` directly).

decisions.rationale: Keeping the Set is the narrowest change that satisfies the brief. The iteration pattern is load-bearing for when more builtins appear; collapsing it now would be a second, unrequested refactor. Three Defaults: narrow scope, preserve shape.

decisions.question: Should the file header docstring at `clerk.ts:11` (`built-in type ('mandate')`) be updated to reference the constant symbolically?
decisions.context: Line 11 is a JSDoc header describing the apparatus. The text `built-in type ('mandate')` is natural-English narration of the name as it appears to users of the apparatus — it does not participate in enforcement. Changing it to e.g. `built-in type (${BUILTIN_WRIT_TYPE})` or a reference would not produce a live link; JSDoc narration would still encode the string by hand.

options.leave-docstring: Leave the JSDoc narration untouched. The text is documentation, not an enforcement site, and will only diverge if the value is renamed — at which point all documentation (including this docstring, `types.ts` JSDocs, and README) is updated together as a coordinated rename pass.
options.reword-docstring: Reword to something like `the built-in default type` (omitting the literal) so the doc never lies even after a rename, at the cost of a reader no longer seeing the concrete name in the header.

decisions.rationale: The brief scopes the change to the two enforcement literals and explicitly flags this is a narrow cleanup. Docstrings that mention the current name are normal documentation practice; a rename commission would sweep them uniformly. Three Defaults: narrow scope.

decisions.question: Should the new constant carry a JSDoc matching the `CASCADE_PARENT_TERMINATION_RESOLUTION` precedent (explaining it as a single source of truth)?
decisions.context: The sibling constant on line 78–84 has a multi-line JSDoc explaining that it is the single source of truth and where it is referenced. A future reader encountering the new constant without a similar explanation would not know why the literal was hoisted rather than left inline.

options.with-jsdoc: Add a JSDoc matching the sibling's style: one paragraph explaining that this is the builtin writ type name, used both as the sole member of `BUILTIN_TYPES` and as the `resolveDefaultType()` fallback, exported as a single source of truth so downstream rename lands in one place.
options.no-jsdoc: Declare without JSDoc. The name and the two call sites are self-explanatory for anyone already in the file.

decisions.rationale: Matches the established sibling pattern in the same file and gives the next reader the same single-source-of-truth signal `CASCADE_PARENT_TERMINATION_RESOLUTION` provides. Three Defaults: extend convention at the right layer rather than abbreviate.

writs.title: Spider's kit-vs-kit mapping precedence is load-order dependent with a silent warning

decisions.question: What enforcement policy should replace the current `console.warn` + first-registered-wins behaviour when two kits contribute a `rigTemplateMappings` entry for the same writ type?
decisions.context: `RigTemplateRegistry.registerKitMappings` at spider.ts:1100-1122 silently picks the first-registered kit and emits a console.warn for the rest. The brief explicitly offers two framings (deterministic rule, or hard failure). Three Defaults #2 (prefer fail-loud to silent fallback) points sharply at the warn being the thing to eliminate.

options.throw: On any kit-vs-kit collision, throw at registration time with a message naming both contributing plugins and the conflicting writ type. No silent resolution. Operator must resolve by dropping one mapping, adding a config-level override, or changing a plugin's `requires` relationship to make the conflict impossible.
options.deterministic-winner: Keep silent resolution but replace load-order with a deterministic rule (see D2). No throw; operators only notice collisions if the chosen winner surprises them.
options.tiered-default-override: Introduce a two-tier kit-contribution model: a kit marks its mapping as either `default` (lowest precedence, last-wins or fallback) or `override` (highest precedence after config, throws on override-vs-override collision). Default-vs-override resolves silently to override. Matches the parent-commission's constraint that Spider's `mandate → default` is a fallback while Astrolabe's `mandate → plan-and-ship` is a specialization.
options.status-quo: Keep warn + first-registered-wins. Do nothing. Rejected up front but listed so the patron can see the full option space.

decisions.rationale: Three Defaults #2 (prefer fail-loud to silent fallback) directly applies to the current warn+skip behaviour, and the brief itself lists "replaces the warning with a hard failure" as one of its two suggested framings. Throw is the smallest, loudest change; more elaborate options (tiered) can be added later if a legitimate overlay pattern emerges.

decisions.question: If D1 chooses `deterministic-winner`, what rule decides which kit wins? (Moot if D1=throw; included so the patron can flip D1 without reopening this decision.)
decisions.context: Two candidate rules exist with meaningful semantics: dependency-based (kit A wins over kit B if A `requires` B, i.e. the specialization wins over its defaults) and alphabetical by pluginId. Alphabetical is simple and total but arbitrary; dependency-based matches the intent of plugin relationships but leaves siblings-with-no-dependency unresolved and therefore still needs a fallback (back to alphabetical, or escalate to throw).

options.dependency-then-throw: If one kit `requires` the other, the requirer wins. Otherwise throw. Uses the relationship the operator already declares and reduces to D1=throw for genuinely ambiguous cases.
options.dependency-then-alphabetical: Same dependency rule first, alphabetical by pluginId as tie-breaker. Fully deterministic, never throws.
options.alphabetical-only: Always alphabetical by pluginId. Simple, total, but ignores the meaningful dependency signal.
options.explicit-priority-field: Add a new `priority` (or similar) field to the kit `rigTemplateMappings` entry; numeric or string ordered. New API surface, but makes the operator-facing contract explicit.

decisions.rationale: If the patron prefers a deterministic-winner path, the dependency graph is the only tie-breaker already expressed in plugin metadata; everything else either invents new API surface or picks arbitrarily. Falling back to throw (rather than alphabetical) keeps Three Defaults #2 alive for the genuinely-ambiguous case.

decisions.question: Should the new precedence rule apply framework-wide (Spider `rigTemplateMappings` + Clerk `writTypes` + Spider `blockTypes` + Fabricator engine designs), or only to the brief's literal subject (`rigTemplateMappings`)?
decisions.context: Every kit-vs-kit merge site in the framework today has a different collision policy — warn+first-wins (rigTemplateMappings, writTypes), silent last-wins (blockTypes, fabricator engines), and throw (clerk.linkKinds). The brief is nominally Spider-scoped but explicitly calls out the general shape of the hazard. Extending the rule now unifies the policy; deferring it keeps the change surface small.

options.spider-only: Apply the chosen rule to `spider.rigTemplateMappings` only. Other merge sites continue with their current (inconsistent) policies. Future commissions address each site individually if and when they bite.
options.framework-wide: Apply the chosen rule to every kit-vs-kit merge site: `rigTemplateMappings`, `writTypes`, `blockTypes`, and fabricator engines. If a framework-level helper emerges (e.g. in `guild-lifecycle.ts`), use it; otherwise replicate the rule in each apparatus.
options.framework-wide-via-helper: Same as framework-wide, but extract the rule into a shared helper in `packages/framework/arbor/src/guild-lifecycle.ts` so future kit-merge registries get consistent behaviour by construction. Largest blast radius; highest long-term payoff.

decisions.rationale: The brief identifies the hazard as a class, not a single instance; a Spider-only fix leaves `blockTypes` and Fabricator engines in a worse state (silent last-wins) than the site being fixed. Extending the rule is the natural way to remove the class of latent bugs rather than one instance. A dedicated helper (framework-wide-via-helper) is recommendable if the rule proves simple enough to factor cleanly — defer that shape choice to the implementer.

decisions.question: Does Spider continue to contribute the plugin-default `rigTemplateMappings: { mandate: 'default' }` kit entry, or does it move to guild-config convention?
decisions.context: Spider's supportKit at spider.ts:2577-2583 contributes `{ mandate: 'default' }` as a plugin-level convenience so guilds with only Spider installed dispatch mandates without extra configuration. The parent commission `w-mobzggpr-d37a4769915f` requires this fallback to continue working: "a guild with Spider installed but not Astrolabe must get the default rig without additional configuration." If D1=throw, removing Spider's default entry is a clean way to make sure Astrolabe is the only kit mapping `mandate` and no collision ever happens. Keeping Spider's entry plus D1=throw means the parent commission must handle the collision some other way (e.g. Astrolabe requires Spider and the rule says the requirer wins, or config-level override in every deployment).

options.keep-with-dependency-rule: Spider keeps `{ mandate: 'default' }`. Astrolabe adds `requires: ['spider']` and the collision rule (D1/D2) gives Astrolabe the win. The parent commission's constraint is satisfied via the precedence rule rather than by removing Spider's entry.
options.drop-from-spider: Spider removes `{ mandate: 'default' }` from its supportKit. Guilds without Astrolabe must put `rigTemplateMappings: { mandate: 'default' }` in their guild.json spider config. Breaks the parent-commission constraint unless the parent is also updated.
options.defer-to-parent: Treat this as entirely the parent commission's decision. This follow-up only codifies the general rule; whichever way the parent resolves its specific collision is orthogonal.
options.out-of-scope: This commission does not touch Spider's default entry; it only changes the collision-detection behaviour. If the new rule throws on the parent's resolved collision, the parent commission is re-opened.

decisions.rationale: The parent brief explicitly scopes the ownership of writ-type-to-rig mappings ("The broader question of which plugin owns writ-type-to-rig mappings ... this commission resolves the specific collision introduced here and does not re-open the general question") — which means this follow-up should not hijack that decision. Recording this decision explicitly guards against the implementer quietly deleting Spider's default mapping to sidestep the collision, which would retroactively rewrite the parent commission's resolution space.

writs.title: `docs/architecture/apparatus/astrolabe.md` carries a stale `refines` link diagram for a mandate that no longer gets posted

decisions.question: Should the `## Writ Linking` section be deleted outright, or rewritten to describe the parent/child relationship observation-lift now produces?
decisions.context: The brief offers both options as equal alternatives. The same astrolabe.md already describes observation-lift's `clerk.post({ type: 'brief', ..., parentId, draft: true })` child-writ fan-out in the Rig Template narration (lines 100–107, 132) and in the Clockwork Engine Designs entry for `astrolabe.observation-lift` (line 156). No other apparatus doc under `docs/architecture/apparatus/` carries a `## Writ Linking` section — the header is idiosyncratic to astrolabe.md, and grep confirms no inbound anchors reference it. Astrolabe itself registers no link kinds today (the `refines` kind was always a documentation-only artifact of the retired `spec-publish` engine).

options.delete: Remove the entire `## Writ Linking` section (header plus prose plus diagram, lines 344–352). Leave no replacement — observation-lift's parent/child behaviour is already documented in the rig narration and engine table.
options.rewrite: Replace the section's contents with a short description of the observation-lift fan-out: draft child `brief` writs created under the originating brief via `parentId`, no named link kind registered. Keep the `## Writ Linking` header as the canonical place to find writ-relationship semantics.

decisions.rationale: Removing matches codebase convention (no sibling apparatus doc has a Writ Linking section), eliminates duplication with the existing rig-narration and engine-table descriptions of observation-lift, and honours the `prefer removal to deprecation` default; a rewrite would only restate what the doc already says two paragraphs earlier.

writs.title: `oculus.test.ts` URL-parsing test uses retired writ-type literal `'brief'`

decisions.question: What placeholder replaces the literal `'brief'` at `packages/plugins/oculus/src/oculus.test.ts:316-317`?
decisions.context: The second value in the fixture is opaque to `parseQueryParams` — the test only verifies that repeated `type=` params are collected into an array. The parent commission (`w-mobzggpr-d37a4769915f`) analysed the same question under its D4 and selected `'bug'` with the rationale that an opaque, non-writ-type placeholder keeps future greps for `'brief'` clean and does not imply live vocabulary. `'mandate'` is already the first fixture value, so the placeholder must differ.

options.bug: Rename to `'bug'`. Opaque placeholder, not a live writ type, matches the parent commission's D4 selection exactly. Two sites (URL string + assertion array) change in lockstep.
options.piece: Rename to `'piece'`, the live Astrolabe-contributed writ type. Reads as plausible live vocabulary but risks implying the test is exercising writ-type validity (it is not).
options.task: Rename to `'task'`. Generic placeholder; not a live writ type; no precedent in neighbouring tests.
options.leave-as-is: Leave `'brief'` in place. The unit under test does not fail, but the literal names a writ type the framework no longer registers — the exact grep-hygiene concern the brief (and parent D4) was written to address.

decisions.rationale: Pre-empted: the parent commission's D4 selected `'bug'` and the parent's t4 plans to apply this exact rename. Aligning with the parent keeps the two commissions' merge orders order-independent — whichever lands first produces the same final state.

decisions.question: What does the implementer do if the parent commission (`w-mobzggpr-d37a4769915f`) has already applied the rename to `'bug'` before this commission runs?
decisions.context: The parent commission's t4 enumerates the exact same single-line edit that constitutes this commission's entire scope. Commissions can merge in either order. If the parent merges first, the Oculus test will already read `'bug'` at lines 316-317 when this commission's implement engine opens the file.

options.no-op-seal: Detect the target state (both occurrences already read `'bug'`) and seal as a no-op with a brief note in the commit/PR description explaining overlap with the parent commission. No code change.
options.fail-loud: Throw if the file does not match the expected pre-state. Forces an explicit human decision.
options.redundant-edit: Apply the rename anyway as a no-op edit. Produces an empty diff; clutters history.

decisions.rationale: Three Defaults: prefer removal to deprecation — the commission's stated scope is already satisfied, so the correct action is to close cleanly rather than invent work. Fail-loud would be appropriate if the post-state were uncertain, but here an already-renamed literal is precisely the intended end state. The implementer should verify the file is in the target shape and seal the commission with an explicit note.

decisions.question: Does this commission extend beyond the single literal at lines 316-317 to audit other stale test-literal vocabulary in the Oculus package (or more broadly)?
decisions.context: The brief body flags an 'ergonomic value of routinely aligning test-literal vocabulary with live vocabulary' — a policy observation that could imply a broader audit. The brief title and concrete body text name exactly one file and one test. The parent commission's S5 already covers the rest of the repo's `'brief'` test-fixture sweep.

options.narrow-to-named-literal: Scope stops at the two textual occurrences of `'brief'` on lines 316-317 of `oculus.test.ts`. Broader vocabulary hygiene is a future concern and does not ride along.
options.audit-oculus-package: Grep the entire `packages/plugins/oculus` tree for any retired-vocabulary test literals and clean them up in this commission.
options.audit-repo-wide: Grep the entire monorepo and clean up all retired-vocabulary test literals here.

decisions.rationale: Brief prescribes one file and one literal; the parent commission already covers the broader sweep under its own S5. Expanding scope here would overlap the parent unnecessarily and re-open a vocabulary-hygiene policy question that the brief only flagged as a meta-observation, not a goal. The Oculus package grep (`packages/plugins/oculus` — verified in inventory) has exactly one `'brief'` hit, which is the named literal; there is no hidden cleanup to pick up even if the scope were expanded.

decisions.question: What exactly changes in Astrolabe's `supportKit.writTypes` array?
decisions.context: Today `supportKit.writTypes` (`packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/astrolabe.ts:278-281`) declares two entries: `brief` and `piece`. The brief explicitly removes `brief` and keeps `piece` as load-bearing for mandate decomposition. The shape of `writTypes` is `{ name, description? }[]` and registration is first-wins.

options.remove-brief-keep-piece: Drop the brief entry; leave piece unchanged. Net: writTypes length 2 → 1.
options.remove-both: Drop both brief and piece, making Astrolabe contribute no writ types. Out of scope per brief (piece is load-bearing for the piece-based task manifest).
options.remove-brief-rename-description: Drop brief; also rewrite piece's description to reflect the type-collapse context. Unnecessary churn.

decisions.rationale: Brief prescribes this exactly: 'Astrolabe's supportKit.writTypes drops the brief entry. The piece entry stays'.

decisions.question: How is the Spider vs Astrolabe `mandate` kit-mapping collision resolved so the winner is deterministic and a Spider-only guild still dispatches mandates to `default` with no additional configuration?
decisions.context: After S2, Astrolabe contributes `mandate: 'astrolabe.plan-and-ship'` as a kit mapping; Spider contributes `mandate: 'default'` as a kit mapping (`packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts:2577-2583`). The current kit-vs-kit precedence is 'first-registered-wins with warning' (`packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts:1113-1118`), and `wireKitEntries` orders apparatuses by topo-sort of their `requires` only. Astrolabe `recommends` Spider but does not `require` it, so topo sort does not force an order — which kit registers first depends on guild.json plugin declaration order. The brief lists three candidate mechanisms (explicit kit priority, config-level override convention, Spider dropping its default entirely) but frames them all as 'the implementer's call.'

options.A-spider-drops-mapping-lookup-fallback: Spider drops its `mandate: 'default'` kit mapping entirely. Spider's `lookup()` gains a final fallback rule: when the writ type is the sole builtin (`mandate`), no config mapping exists, no kit mapping exists, and a `default` (or `spider.default`) template is registered, use it. Astrolabe's kit mapping wins by having no competition; Spider-only guilds still get the default rig via the new lookup fallback.
options.B-dependency-based-precedence: Keep both kit mappings. Change Spider's `registerKitMappings` policy so that when two kits register the same writ type, the kit whose `requires`/`recommends` set includes the other's plugin id wins. Astrolabe recommends Spider → Astrolabe's mapping wins deterministically regardless of registration order.
options.C-flip-to-last-wins-plus-require: Upgrade Astrolabe's `recommends: ['spider']` to `requires: ['spider']` so topo sort always places Spider before Astrolabe. Flip Spider's kit-vs-kit precedence from first-wins to last-wins. Astrolabe, registered after Spider, wins deterministically. Affects every other kit collision too.
options.D-explicit-priority-field: Extend the kit mapping shape with an optional `priority` field (higher wins; default 0; Spider declares priority 0; Astrolabe declares something higher). Registry compares priorities deterministically; unlisted priorities default to first-wins among equals.
options.E-config-override-convention: Keep both kit mappings. Document that any guild installing Astrolabe must declare `spider.rigTemplateMappings.mandate = 'astrolabe.plan-and-ship'` in guild.json to override the collision. Violates the 'without additional configuration' constraint for Astrolabe guilds — listed for completeness; not viable.

decisions.rationale: Option A is the smallest change that satisfies both constraints: the collision is eliminated at the contribution layer (not papered over with policy), so there is no registration-order dependency to reason about. Spider-only guilds still dispatch via an explicit lookup fallback tied to the single builtin writ type (`mandate`) and Spider's existing default template — both already treated specially in the codebase. Option B is elegant but smuggles semantic meaning into `recommends` that it does not carry elsewhere; Option C touches every cross-kit collision policy to fix one instance; Option D adds API surface the brief wants avoided ('this commission resolves the specific collision ... does not re-open the general question'); Option E is ruled out by the brief's constraint.

decisions.question: Assuming Option A in D2, what is the precise fallback rule in `lookup()`?
decisions.context: If Spider drops its kit mapping for `mandate`, the `mandate` builtin needs a deterministic fallback so Spider-only guilds still dispatch to the `default` template without configuration. The rule must be narrow enough not to resurrect the 'every unmapped type falls back to default' pattern the codebase deliberately retired (`packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.test.ts:5219-5239` locks in 'opt-in dispatch: unmapped writ types are inert').

options.narrow-mandate-only: Fallback applies only to the hardcoded builtin `mandate` writ type. Other unmapped types remain inert. The lookup rule reads: 'if writType === "mandate" and no config/kit mapping exists and a template named "default" (or the Spider-qualified "spider.default") is registered, return it.'
options.broad-default-fallback: Any unmapped writ type falls back to a registered `default` template. Resurrects a previously retired behavior; would break the existing 'unmapped is inert' test.
options.config-sourced-fallback: Spider introduces a new config field (e.g. `spider.fallbackTemplate`) that guild ops set explicitly. Shifts burden to guild config — violates 'no additional configuration' for Spider-only guilds.

decisions.rationale: Targeted fallback preserves the opt-in-dispatch contract for every non-builtin type and couples the behavior to the only type that is builtin in the Clerk. The rule has a single hardcoded reference — `'mandate'` — symmetric with how the Clerk treats `mandate` in `BUILTIN_TYPES`.

decisions.question: What is done about `packages/plugins/oculus/src/oculus.test.ts:316-318` (URL-parsing test that uses the literal string `'brief'` in a query string)?
decisions.context: The test parses `?type=mandate&type=brief` to confirm that repeated params with an array-accepting zod schema parse as an array. It is testing query-string parsing, not writ-type registration; `'brief'` is opaque to the unit under test. It would not break after the commission, but the string is stale.

options.leave-as-is: Leave `'brief'` as a meaningless string literal in the test; it is not asserting anything about writ-type validity.
options.rename-to-bug: Rename the second value to a generic placeholder like `'bug'` or `'task'` so the test does not reference a retired writ type.
options.rename-to-piece: Rename the second value to `'piece'` (a live Astrolabe-contributed writ type) so any future reader can read the literal as a real type.

decisions.rationale: The test value should not cite retired terminology that the reader might assume reflects current state. A generic string (`'bug'` matches the neighboring test on line 206) keeps the unit under test parseable without implying live vocabulary. Trivial diff; zero behavioral risk.

decisions.question: What happens to English-prose 'brief writ' phrasing in code comments, docstrings, and narrative documentation?
decisions.context: Many source comments, engine docstrings, and architecture-doc sentences use 'brief writ' as shorthand for 'the patron's originating writ' — e.g., 'the brief writ reaches completed only after seal succeeds.' Per the brief, 'English brief is not what's removed' and sage-role instructions/trigger vocabulary are untouched. But after the typed identifier goes away, these phrases no longer refer to anything named in the code, and a reader encountering 'brief writ' in a comment would reasonably assume a typed concept still exists. Cleanup is optional polish, not functional.

options.full-cleanup: Rewrite every 'brief writ' comment/docstring/narrative occurrence to 'mandate writ' (or just 'writ' where type is irrelevant), keeping sage-role instructions and trigger-vocabulary docs untouched per brief.
options.tactical-only: Update only phrases that directly border the typed identifier (e.g. 'creates one draft brief writ per record' in observation-lift's docstring, where code once posted `type: 'brief'`). Leave general narrative phrasing ('keyed by the brief writ ID', 'the brief writ reaches completed') alone — the English sense of 'brief' as patron's prose is still fine.
options.leave-alone: Do nothing. The brief says English brief is untouched; treat 'brief writ' as an English phrase that still reads correctly if you read 'brief' as 'initial' or 'short-form'.

decisions.rationale: Three Defaults: 'Prefer removal to deprecation' — but the scope line is the brief's own carve-out ('English brief is not what's removed'). Tactical-only lands every place the phrase is next to a now-invalid type literal (so the reader is not misled at the site where the code change happens) while respecting the explicit non-goal of rewriting the project's English register. Full-cleanup pushes beyond the brief's intent; leave-alone misses the co-located cases where the comment would actively disagree with the code diff.

decisions.question: How is the `### Writ Types (contributed to Clerk)` section in `packages/plugins/astrolabe/README.md` (lines 121-125) handled after `brief` is removed?
decisions.context: The table today has one row: `brief`. After removal, the table has no rows. Astrolabe still contributes the `piece` writ type via supportKit, but it is not currently listed in README.md's `Writ Types` section (either because the doc was never updated for piece, or because the doc was written before piece was added).

options.delete-section: Delete the entire `### Writ Types (contributed to Clerk)` section. Astrolabe-contributed writ types become undocumented in the README (piece remains in code but undocumented here).
options.retain-with-piece: Replace the brief row with a row for piece, documenting the writ type Astrolabe still contributes. Surfaces the existing piece contribution; requires a one-line description that matches the code.
options.retain-empty-with-note: Keep the section header; replace the row with a note such as 'Astrolabe no longer contributes writ types directly; the combined rig operates on the Clerk's builtin `mandate` type.' Ignores the live piece contribution.

decisions.rationale: The README should reflect what Astrolabe actually contributes today. Piece is a real contribution (`packages/plugins/astrolabe/src/astrolabe.ts:280`) — documenting it costs nothing and corrects an existing doc gap surfaced by this commission. Delete-section drops the gap silently; retain-empty-with-note under-documents reality.

writs.title: Astrolabe structured observations and draft-writ emission

decisions.question: What fields does the Observation record carry?
decisions.context: The brief's shape sketch is `{ id: 'obs-1', title: '...', body: '...' }` and explicitly names `id`, `title`, `body` as the minimum. No other fields are mentioned (no status, no addressedBy, no timestamps). PlanDoc already has its own createdAt/updatedAt.

options.minimum: Exactly {id, title, body}. No timestamps on individual observations.
options.withTimestamps: Add createdAt/updatedAt per observation for future diffing.
options.withTags: Add an optional `kind` or tags array to categorise refactor/bug/doc-drift etc.

decisions.rationale: Brief's shape sketch is authoritative and explicitly minimal: 'at minimum a plandoc-local id, a title, and a body. No status field. No addressedBy.' Pre-emption.

decisions.question: Should the `observations-write` tool param accept only the new Observation[] shape, or accept either the new shape or the legacy string?
decisions.context: Today the tool takes `observations: z.string()`. Brief's out-of-scope list: 'Schema evolution for existing plandocs … [is out of scope]. If any existing plandocs carry the old string shape, they're left alone — the structured shape applies to new plandocs only.' That covers reads; writes still have to pick.

options.strict: Zod schema = `z.array(z.object({ id, title, body }))`. Writes of the old prose shape are rejected at tool-param validation time.
options.union: Zod schema accepts either the array or a legacy string, tool handler normalises.

decisions.rationale: Brief is explicit that the new shape is the shape; Three Defaults rule 1 (prefer removal to deprecation) and rule 2 (prefer fail-loud) both land on strict.

decisions.question: Who generates `Observation.id` — the sage role writing the record, or the tool / engine?
