decisions.context: Brief: 'Ids are stable within a plandoc. Cross-plandoc uniqueness is not required.' The sketch uses `obs-1`, `obs-2` — suggestive but not prescriptive. Downstream lift does not depend on the id format.

options.sageAssigned: Sage emits ids in the array (convention: `obs-1`, `obs-2`, …). Tool stores verbatim.
options.toolGenerated: Tool generates sequential ids `obs-N` from array index; sage omits the field.
options.slugFromTitle: Tool derives id from the title (kebab-case slug).

decisions.rationale: Matches the brief's shape sketch exactly, keeps the tool a thin pass-through like the other write tools, and the sage already emits ids on ScopeItem and Decision arrays — same pattern.

decisions.question: Should the observations-write tool validate that ids are unique within the array, and that title/body are non-empty?
decisions.context: The tool today validates basic types via zod. Decision, ScopeItem arrays are not id-uniqueness-checked at tool layer — downstream consumers (decision-review engine) happen to blow up on duplicates instead. Observation ids are not read by downstream code; only the post-planning engine enumerates them.

options.zodOnly: Only enforce type shape via zod. Non-empty strings via `z.string().min(1)`. No id-uniqueness check.
options.validateUnique: Add a zod refine() step verifying ids are unique within the array.
options.noValidation: Use `z.string()` without min(1), accept duplicate ids silently.

decisions.rationale: Matches how decisions-write / scope-write behave today (type shape only) — the apparatus defers uniqueness enforcement to consumers. Three Defaults rule 2 (fail-loud) still applies on the required fields (non-empty strings).

decisions.question: How does the astrolabe detail page render the new Observation[] shape in the Observations tab?
decisions.context: Page already has precedent: `renderScopeTable(scope)` and `renderDecisionsTable(decisions)` for structured arrays; `renderMarkdown(plan.observations)` for the current string. Observations have a short title + markdown body, which doesn't fit a tight table cell well (body is multi-line markdown).

options.cardList: New `renderObservations(observations)` helper: iterate records, emit a titled section per observation with the body rendered through `renderMarkdown`.
options.table: Render as a two-column table (title | body), with body rendered as markdown inside the cell.
options.concatenated: Concatenate records into a single markdown string (e.g. `## title\n\nbody`) and pass through `renderMarkdown`.

decisions.rationale: Body is markdown — card-list layout matches the body's shape and mirrors how decisions' details rows already render. A table would squeeze the body; concatenation obscures the per-record structure in the DOM.

decisions.question: Which sage roles get their instructions updated for the new observation shape?
decisions.context: `observations-write` is referenced by sage-primer-scoping, sage-primer-solo, sage-primer-attended, and sage-writer (gap-report path). sage-primer-reader only reads the field's description in plan-show — no write path.

options.allFourWriters: Update sage-primer-scoping, sage-primer-solo, sage-primer-attended, and sage-writer.
options.primersOnly: Update only the three primer variants; leave sage-writer's gap-report prose alone.
options.allFive: Update the four writers plus sage-primer-reader (just the plan-show field-description bullet).

decisions.rationale: Tool signature is now strict-array, so every code path that calls observations-write (including sage-writer gap reports) has to match. Primer-reader's change would be cosmetic (it doesn't write) and ties the commission to stylistic doc churn not tied to the shape change.

decisions.question: Does sage-writer's gap-report semantic (blocking the pipeline when decisions don't cover the design space) change, or only the shape of what it writes?
decisions.context: sage-writer Step 2 uses observations-write to log 'gaps that must be resolved before a spec can be written,' then refuses to write a spec. That causes plan-finalize to throw and the rig to fail. Distinct from primer observations (which are follow-up ideas, not blockers).

options.shapeOnly: Preserve the blocking semantic; just have sage-writer emit structured records (title: gap summary, body: what's missing). Post-planning engine never runs in this failure path anyway.
options.semanticChange: Split gap reports into a separate field or mechanism so they don't share a field with follow-up observations.

decisions.rationale: Brief is explicit that only the packaging changes: 'The only content change is one-record-per-concern and the title.' Any semantic rework would be a new design. Three Defaults rule 3 (extend at the right layer) — if gap-reports need their own field in future, that's a separate design.

decisions.question: What guidance do the updated role instructions give on observation title content (length, style)?
decisions.context: The title becomes the writ's title downstream. Writ titles are 'short human-readable title describing the work' by Clerk convention. The sage already produces scope item and decision titles at this level of concision.

options.oneLine: Guidance: one line, commission-title style (imperative or noun phrase, ~10 words max, no trailing punctuation). Matches existing patron brief writ titles.
options.open: No specific guidance; sage uses its own judgement.

decisions.rationale: Title becomes a writ title downstream; shaping it consistently with existing writ titles reduces friction for the overseer that will curate these. Three Defaults rule 2 (fail-loud is downstream-context-appropriate) — giving the sage an explicit contract avoids silent style drift.

decisions.question: What is the new engine's designId?
decisions.context: Existing engines use `astrolabe.<noun-phrase>` convention: `astrolabe.plan-init`, `astrolabe.inventory-check`, `astrolabe.decision-review`, `astrolabe.spec-publish`, `astrolabe.plan-finalize`. The brief and its design click (`c-moaz1pdw`) use the phrase 'observation-lift' / 'draft-writ emission' / 'post-planning step'.
decisions.rationale: Matches the brief's vocabulary exactly ('observation-lift post-planning step'), reads naturally in rig-template listings, and keeps symmetry with existing kebab-case engine ids.

decisions.question: Is the new engine clockwork or quick (anima-backed)?
decisions.context: All comparable engines (plan-init, inventory-check, spec-publish, plan-finalize, decision-review) are clockwork. The lift step is a mechanical walk-and-post operation; no judgement needed.

options.clockwork: Pure clockwork: implements EngineDesign.run(), returns `{ status: 'completed', yields }`.
options.quick: Anima-backed: launches a session via animator.

decisions.rationale: Brief describes a deterministic mechanical step; no anima needed. Matches every sibling engine of similar shape.

decisions.question: What preconditions does the new engine validate before lifting observations?
decisions.context: Sibling engines validate plan existence and status. inventory-check requires status=reading, plan-finalize requires status=writing + non-empty spec, spec-publish requires status=writing + non-empty spec.

options.statusAndPlan: Require plan exists AND plan.status === 'completed'. (In all three rigs, the preceding engine — plan-finalize or spec-publish — has already set status to 'completed'.)
options.planOnly: Require plan exists; ignore status. Let the walk-and-post succeed on any plan the engine is given.
options.noValidation: Trust the rig; no explicit validation, just read and lift.

decisions.rationale: Three Defaults rule 2 (fail-loud): validate what the rig contract depends on. Matches sibling engine pattern (inventory-check / plan-finalize / spec-publish all validate status).

decisions.question: What does the engine yield on success?
decisions.context: inventory-check yields `{}`, spec-publish yields `{ generatedWritId }`, plan-finalize yields `{ spec }`. Nothing downstream in the rig templates consumes observation-lift's yield, but rig/engine yields are recorded for debugging and also serve as the engine's audit trail.

options.writIds: Yield `{ writIds: string[] }` — the ids of the observation writs created.
options.detailed: Yield `{ observationsLifted: number, writIds: string[] }` — count + ids.

decisions.rationale: Matches spec-publish's precedent of yielding the created writ id(s); traceability in rig debugging is cheap. Count is redundant with the array length.

decisions.question: How does the engine handle a failure partway through lifting multiple observations (e.g. the third clerk.post() throws)?
decisions.context: Each clerk.post call is a separate transaction. Already-lifted writs are persisted independently. The rig's stuck path would surface the engine error to the patron.

options.failFast: Throw on the first failure. Previously-created writs remain as orphaned drafts (recoverable by the patron).
options.continueAndAggregate: Catch per-observation errors, continue, and aggregate them into a single thrown error at the end.
options.rollback: Attempt to undo previously-created writs on failure.

decisions.rationale: Three Defaults rule 2 (fail-loud). Rollback is complex for little benefit — the orphaned drafts are in `new` status, invisible to Spider, trivially cancellable by the patron. Rig goes stuck, patron investigates; normal failure flow.

decisions.question: How does the observation writ inherit its codex — explicit `codex: plan.codex` or rely on Clerk's parentId-inheritance?
decisions.context: `clerk.post` with `parentId` set and `codex` omitted inherits codex from parent. spec-publish passes codex explicitly from `plan.codex`. Both paths produce the same result when parent.codex === plan.codex (which it always does — the plan's codex is set from the brief writ's codex at plan-init).

options.explicit: Pass `codex: plan.codex` on every clerk.post call.
options.inherit: Omit codex; let Clerk inherit from parent writ.

decisions.rationale: Matches spec-publish precedent (the closest peer engine). Makes the engine self-contained and diagnostic-friendly — reading the engine alone tells you what codex observation writs land in.

decisions.question: What does the engine do if it encounters a plan with the legacy `observations: string` shape instead of the new array shape?
decisions.context: Brief out-of-scope: 'A plandoc with the old string-shaped observations field is not processed by the new step (no lift, no error). The step only recognizes the structured array shape.'

options.silentSkip: Type-check `Array.isArray(plan.observations)`; if false (including the string case), skip silently with empty yield.
options.throwOnLegacy: Throw when observations is a string — unexpected state post-migration.
options.convertInPlace: Attempt to parse the string into observations and lift.

decisions.rationale: Pre-empted by the brief's behavioral-case list. Specifically called out: 'no lift, no error. The step only recognizes the structured array shape.'

decisions.question: What does the engine do when observations is an empty array or undefined?
decisions.context: Brief: 'A plandoc with zero observations (empty array or missing field) produces no writs and no errors.'

options.noOp: Return `{ status: 'completed', yields: { writIds: [] } }` with no side effects.
options.throwOnMissing: Throw when the field is absent (fail-loud on missing upstream output).

decisions.rationale: Pre-empted by the brief's behavioral-case list. Empty observations is a legitimate state (the primer had nothing to note), not a pipeline failure.

decisions.question: Which rig templates get the new engine — plan-and-ship only, or all three including the legacy two-phase/three-phase templates?
decisions.context: plan-and-ship is the default rig for `brief` writs. two-phase and three-phase are opt-in via guild config override, preserved for backward compat. All three accumulate observations into the plandoc through the primer / scoping stage. Brief: 'This work touches only Astrolabe' and doesn't carve out any rig.

options.allThree: Add observation-lift to plan-and-ship, two-phase-planning, and three-phase-planning.
options.planAndShipOnly: Add only to plan-and-ship; leave the legacy rigs as they are.
options.splitRigs: Add to plan-and-ship now; handle the legacy rigs in a follow-up.

decisions.rationale: Users running the legacy rigs via config override expect consistent observation-lift behaviour; there's no reason behaviour should silently diverge. Three Defaults rule 3 (extend the API at the right layer) — the feature is a property of the planning pipeline, not of one specific rig. Cost: two extra one-line rig edits.

decisions.question: Where in the plan-and-ship rig does the observation-lift engine sit?
decisions.context: plan-and-ship engine order today: plan-init → draft → reader-analyst → inventory-check → patron-anima → decision-review → spec-writer → plan-finalize → implement → review → revise → seal. The brief writ stays in `open` phase until `seal` completes (which is when the rig transitions to completed and Spider transitions the writ to completed via CDC). Child writs can only be created under `new`/`open`/`stuck` parents. seal is the resolutionEngine.

options.afterPlanFinalize: Insert between `plan-finalize` and `implement`. Upstream: [plan-finalize]. Implement's upstream shifts to [observation-lift].
options.beforeSeal: Insert between `revise` and `seal`. Upstream: [revise]. Seal's upstream shifts to [observation-lift].
options.afterSpecWriter: Insert between `spec-writer` and `plan-finalize`. Upstream: [spec-writer]. Plan-finalize's upstream shifts to [observation-lift].

decisions.rationale: Brief says 'After the spec-writer completes and the plandoc reaches its final state'; plan-finalize is what marks the plandoc 'final' (status → completed). Placing it before `implement` also runs the lift before the long implementation tail, so operators see the drafts sooner and an implement-phase failure doesn't block observation-lift. Brief writ is still `open` here; child writs are accepted.

decisions.question: Where in the two-phase and three-phase rigs does the observation-lift engine sit?
decisions.context: two-phase: spec-writer → spec-publish → seal (abandon:true). three-phase: …spec-writer → spec-publish → seal (abandon:true). resolutionEngine in both is `spec-writer`, but the rig only reaches status=completed after every engine finishes — so the writ transition still fires at the end. seal with abandon:true doesn't merge anything but does drop the draft worktree.

options.beforeSeal: Between `spec-publish` and `seal`. Upstream: [spec-publish]. Seal's upstream shifts to [observation-lift].
options.afterSpecWriter: Between `spec-writer` and `spec-publish`. Upstream: [spec-writer]. Spec-publish's upstream shifts to [observation-lift].
options.afterSeal: After `seal`. Would attempt child creation under a completed parent — would fail.

decisions.rationale: Brief writ is still `open` before seal; child creation works. Placing observation-lift after spec-publish keeps the lift distinct from the main spec publication, mirrors the 'after the plandoc reaches final state' positioning in plan-and-ship, and keeps the spec-publish → seal tail unaltered except by one new node in between.

decisions.question: Does the engine patch the plandoc with any tracking field (e.g. `observationWritIds`) after lifting?
decisions.context: Brief: 'No special link types, no new writ types, no cross-substrate bookkeeping' and 'Observation lifecycle tracking beyond standard writ lifecycle [is out of scope].'

options.noMutation: Engine does not mutate the plan. The created writs are the sole record.
options.writIdsField: Add `observationWritIds?: string[]` to PlanDoc and populate it from the engine for audit.
options.perObservationRef: Extend Observation with a `writId?` field and backfill it on lift.

decisions.rationale: Pre-empted by brief's explicit out-of-scope: 'No cross-substrate bookkeeping.' Writs exist in Clerk with parentId pointing at the plan id — the relationship is already queryable via clerk.list({ parentId }).

decisions.question: Does the engine only count Observation records that have non-empty title AND non-empty body, or does it attempt to lift every record in the array regardless?
decisions.context: Under D4 we pre-validate non-empty at the tool layer, so in normal flow this can't happen. But the engine reads from the book, which could in theory hold anything persisted through some other path.

options.trustTool: Engine lifts whatever is in the array. Tool validation is the single source of truth; engine is the cheap consumer.
options.defensive: Engine also validates non-empty title/body; throws on invalid records.
options.skipInvalid: Engine silently skips records with empty title or body and lifts the rest.

decisions.rationale: Matches how other clockwork engines treat their upstream: trust the writing contract, validate only rig-contract preconditions (plan status). Three Defaults rule 2 is already satisfied at the tool boundary — re-validating would be defense-in-depth for no benefit.

decisions.question: Does the rig-template entry for observation-lift use `when:` guards or run unconditionally?
decisions.context: Spider rig-template engine entries accept an optional `when` condition. Running unconditionally is simpler; the engine itself already no-ops on empty observations (D16) and on legacy string shape (D15).

options.unconditional: No `when` guard. Engine always runs; handles empty/legacy cases internally.
options.whenNonEmpty: Add a `when` predicate keyed off plandoc shape.

decisions.rationale: Engine's internal branching for empty/legacy is already defined; a `when` guard would duplicate the check at the rig layer. Matches how every existing astrolabe engine is wired.

decisions.question: Which documentation files get updated as part of this commission?
decisions.context: docs/architecture/apparatus/astrolabe.md and packages/plugins/astrolabe/README.md both describe the PlanDoc schema, the observations-write tool contract, and the engine / rig-template list — all of which change here. The sage role .md files are a separate channel (handled under S2).

options.readmeOnly: Update only the README (package-level); leave the architecture doc for a later pass.
options.none: Ship the code change now; defer docs.

decisions.rationale: Both files describe the exact contract this commission changes; leaving them stale would mislead the next sage planning against this apparatus. Three Defaults rule 1 (prefer removal to deprecation) — do the doc refresh in lockstep rather than accumulate two stale-doc points.
